questions.question:
Consider a simple undirected unweighted graph with at least three vertices. If A is the adjacency matrix of the graph, then the number of 3-cycles in the graph is given by the trace of:

A. A^3
B. A^3 divided by 2
C. A^3 divided by 3
D. A^3 divided by 6
questions.to_find: Expression for number of 3-cycles using the adjacency matrix

understand.plain: The entry (A^k)_{ii} counts the number of closed walks of length k starting and ending at vertex i. For 3-cycles, a walk of length 3 that returns to its start is counted once for each vertex it visits, and in each of two directions, so trace(A^3) over-counts each 3-cycle by a factor of 6 (3 starting vertices × 2 directions).

keywords.explain: A sequence of k edges that starts and ends at the same vertex; (A^k)_{ii} counts exactly these from vertex i.
keywords.example: In a triangle ABC, the walk A-B-C-A is one closed walk of length 3 from A.

keywords.explain: The sum of the diagonal entries of a square matrix; trace(A^3) = sum_i (A^3)_{ii}, which sums closed walks of length 3 over all starting vertices.
keywords.example: For a 3-cycle on vertices {1,2,3}, trace(A^3) = 6, giving 6/6 = 1 cycle.

keywords.explain: Each simple 3-cycle is traversed 3 times (once per starting vertex) and in 2 directions, so each cycle contributes 6 to trace(A^3).
keywords.example: Triangle {1,2,3} is counted as walks 1-2-3-1, 2-3-1-2, 3-1-2-3, plus their reverses.

understand.visual_alt: The diagonal entries of A^3 count closed walks of length 3 at each vertex; dividing their total by 6 yields the number of distinct triangles.

given.aim: Find which expression involving the adjacency matrix gives the number of 3-cycles in a simple undirected unweighted graph.

terms.meaning: Adjacency matrix of the graph; A[i][j] = 1 if edge (i,j) exists, else 0
terms.example: For a triangle on vertices 1,2,3: A = [[0,1,1],[1,0,1],[1,1,0]]
terms.connects: A^3 encodes closed walks of length 3

terms.meaning: Number of closed walks of length 3 starting and ending at vertex i
terms.example: For vertex 1 in a triangle: (A^3)_{11} = 2 (walks 1-2-3-1 and 1-3-2-1)
terms.connects: Summing over all i gives trace(A^3)

terms.meaning: A simple cycle visiting exactly 3 distinct vertices (a triangle)

given.plan: Use the closed-walk trace formula: trace(A^3) counts all closed walks of length 3. Each 3-cycle is counted 6 times (3 start vertices x 2 directions), so number of 3-cycles = trace(A^3) / 6.

steps.title: Closed-walk counting via trace
steps.formula_raw: number of closed walks of length k = trace(A^k) = sum_i (A^k)_{ii}
steps.apply: trace(A^3) = sum over all vertices i of (A^3)_{ii} = total number of closed walks of length 3.
steps.note: This counts sequences (u, v, w, u) where edges u-v, v-w, w-u all exist; the graph being undirected means both directions are valid.

steps.title: Account for overcounting in each 3-cycle
steps.formula_raw: each 3-cycle contributes 6 = 3 * 2 closed walks to trace(A^3)
steps.apply: A 3-cycle {u,v,w} generates 6 walks: u-v-w-u, u-w-v-u (two from u); v-w-u-v, v-u-w-v (two from v); w-u-v-w, w-v-u-w (two from w). So number of 3-cycles = trace(A^3) / 6.
steps.note: The factor 3 comes from the 3 choices of starting vertex; the factor 2 comes from the two traversal directions.

questions.formula_note: The closed-walk trace formula (trace(A^k) = number of closed walks of length k) is the key tool; dividing by 6 corrects for the three starting vertices and two directions each 3-cycle generates.

questions.question:
Which of the properties hold for the adjacency matrix A of a simple undirected unweighted graph having n vertices?

A. The diagonal entries of A^2 are the degrees of the vertices of the graph.
B. If the graph is connected, then none of the entries of A^(m-1) can be zero.
C. If the sum of all the elements of A^m is at most n+10 for some m, then the graph must be acyclic.
D. If there is at least a 1 in each of A's rows and columns, then the graph must be connected.
questions.to_find: Which properties of adjacency matrix powers are always true

understand.plain: The diagonal entry (A^2)_{ii} equals the degree of vertex i, because it counts length-2 closed walks at i which correspond exactly to going out along one edge and returning along the same edge. The other options have counterexamples: a connected graph can have entries of A^(n-1) equal to zero, a graph can be cyclic with small row/column sums, and a graph where each row/column has a 1 can still be disconnected.

keywords.explain: The diagonal entries of A^2 count length-2 closed walks at each vertex, which equals the number of neighbours (since you step to any neighbour and return).
keywords.example: A degree-3 vertex has (A^2)_{ii} = 3 since there are 3 distinct length-2 closed walks.

keywords.explain: Two vertices not connected by a path of length exactly m-1 would yield a zero entry in A^(m-1), even if the graph is connected.
keywords.example: A path graph P_4 has (A^2)_{14} = 0 although it is connected.

understand.visual_alt: The diagonal of A squared captures vertex degrees; options B, C, D each fail via counterexamples.

given.aim: Identify which properties of adjacency matrix powers hold for any simple undirected unweighted graph.

terms.meaning: n x n adjacency matrix, A[i][j] = 1 iff edge (i,j) exists
terms.connects: A^k encodes closed and open walks of length k

terms.meaning: Count of length-2 closed walks starting and ending at vertex i
terms.example: Each neighbour of i contributes exactly one such walk (go out and return)
terms.connects: Equals deg(i) — the degree of vertex i

terms.meaning: Diagonal entries of A^2 equal vertex degrees
terms.connects: This is always true by definition of matrix multiplication and adjacency

given.plan: Verify option A using the matrix product formula. Then find counterexamples for B, C, D to eliminate them.

steps.title: Verify option A: (A^2)_{ii} = deg(i)
steps.apply: (A^2)_{ii} = sum_k A[i][k] * A[k][i] = sum_k A[i][k]^2 = sum_k A[i][k] = deg(i), since A[i][k] in {0,1} so A[i][k]^2 = A[i][k].
steps.note: This always holds; the diagonal of A^2 is exactly the degree sequence.

steps.title: Eliminate options B, C, D via counterexamples
steps.apply: B: The path P_4 is connected but (A^2)_{1,4}=0, showing A^(n-1) can have zeros. C: A cycle of length 4 has sum of entries in A^m bounded but is cyclic. D: Two disjoint edges give each vertex degree 1 (so each row/column has a 1) but the graph is disconnected.
steps.note: Only option A is always true.

solution.result: Only option A is always true: the diagonal entries of A^2 equal the degrees of the vertices.

questions.formula_note: The formula (A^2)_{ii} = deg(i) is the key insight: each neighbour of i contributes one length-2 closed walk back to i, so the diagonal of A^2 is precisely the degree sequence.

questions.question: The number of spanning trees in a complete graph of 4 vertices labelled A, B, C, and D is _________.
questions.to_find: Number of spanning trees in K_4

understand.plain: Cayley's Formula tells us that the number of labelled spanning trees of a complete graph K_n is n^(n-2). For n=4, this gives 4^(4-2) = 4^2 = 16. Every spanning tree on 4 vertices has exactly 3 edges and is a tree (connected, acyclic).

keywords.explain: The number of distinct labelled spanning trees of K_n is n^(n-2); it can be proved via Prüfer sequences, which are in bijection with labelled trees.
keywords.example: K_3 has 3^(3-2) = 3 spanning trees: the three different paths on 3 labelled vertices.

keywords.explain: A subgraph that is a tree and includes every vertex of the original graph; on n vertices it has exactly n-1 edges.
keywords.example: On K_4 with vertices A,B,C,D: a star from A to each of B,C,D is one spanning tree.

understand.visual_svg: <svg viewBox='0 0 320 200' xmlns='http://www.w3.org/2000/svg'><style>.bg{fill:var(--cell-bg,#F1EFE8);stroke:var(--cell-line,#B4B2A9);stroke-width:2;}.hi{fill:var(--hi-bg,#CECBF6);stroke:var(--hi-line,#7F77DD);stroke-width:3;}.ok{fill:var(--ok,#1D9E75);}.ink{fill:var(--color-text-primary,#2C2C2A);}.lbl{font-size:18px;font-weight:600;}.sub{font-size:14px;}text{font-family:system-ui,-apple-system,Segoe UI,sans-serif;}</style><rect width='320' height='200' rx='10' class='bg'/><circle cx='80' cy='60' r='20' class='hi'/><circle cx='240' cy='60' r='20' class='hi'/><circle cx='80' cy='150' r='20' class='hi'/><circle cx='240' cy='150' r='20' class='hi'/><line x1='80' y1='60' x2='240' y2='60' stroke='var(--ok,#1D9E75)' stroke-width='2'/><line x1='80' y1='60' x2='80' y2='150' stroke='var(--ok,#1D9E75)' stroke-width='2'/><line x1='80' y1='60' x2='240' y2='150' stroke='var(--ok,#1D9E75)' stroke-width='2'/><text x='80' y='65' text-anchor='middle' class='lbl ink'>A</text><text x='240' y='65' text-anchor='middle' class='lbl ink'>B</text><text x='80' y='155' text-anchor='middle' class='lbl ink'>C</text><text x='240' y='155' text-anchor='middle' class='lbl ink'>D</text><text x='160' y='195' text-anchor='middle' class='sub ink'>One of 16 spanning trees (star from A)</text></svg>
understand.visual_alt: K_4 with vertices A, B, C, D showing one spanning tree as a star from vertex A to the other three vertices.

given.aim: Count the number of spanning trees in the complete graph K_4 on labelled vertices A, B, C, D.

terms.meaning: Complete graph on 4 labelled vertices; every pair of vertices is connected by an edge
terms.connects: We need spanning trees of K_4

terms.meaning: Number of vertices in the graph, here n = 4
terms.connects: Plugged into Cayley's Formula as the base and exponent

terms.meaning: A connected acyclic subgraph that includes all n vertices; has exactly n-1 = 3 edges for K_4
terms.example: Path A-B-C-D, or star A connected to B, C, D
terms.connects: Cayley's formula counts all such distinct labelled trees

given.plan: Apply Cayley's Formula: number of spanning trees of K_n = n^(n-2). Substitute n = 4.

steps.title: Apply Cayley's Formula
steps.formula_raw: number of spanning trees of K_n = n^(n-2)
steps.apply: For K_4 with n = 4: number of spanning trees = 4^(4-2) = 4^2 = 16.
steps.note: Cayley's Formula can be proved via Prüfer sequences; each labelled tree on n vertices corresponds to a unique sequence of length n-2 from the label set.

questions.formula_note: Cayley's Formula n^(n-2) is the single key formula; for K_4 it directly yields 4^2 = 16 spanning trees.

questions.question:
Let G be an undirected connected graph in which every edge has a positive integer weight. Suppose that every spanning tree in G has even weight. Which of the following statements is/are TRUE for every such graph G?

A. All edges in G have even weight OR all edges in G have odd weight
B. All edges in G have even weight
C. All edges in G have odd weight
D. In each cycle C in G, either all edges in C have odd weight OR all edges in C have even weight
questions.to_find: Properties that must hold when every spanning tree of G has even weight

understand.plain: If every spanning tree has even total weight, then swapping any two edges — one from a spanning tree and one from the fundamental cycle that the non-tree edge creates — must keep the weight even. This forces the swap to preserve parity, which means the non-tree edge and the tree edge it replaces must have the same parity. This cascades to show that every cycle must have all edges of the same parity (all odd or all even). Options A, B, C can each be violated by a mixed-parity graph where cycles have consistent parity.

keywords.explain: Adding any non-tree edge e to a spanning tree T creates exactly one cycle; this is the fundamental cycle of e with respect to T.
keywords.example: In a 4-cycle with edges 1-2-3-4-1, a spanning tree T = {1-2, 2-3, 3-4}; adding edge 4-1 creates the fundamental cycle 1-2-3-4-1.

keywords.explain: Replacing a tree edge f with a non-tree edge e changes the tree weight by w(e) - w(f); for every such swap to maintain even total, w(e) and w(f) must have the same parity.
keywords.example: If all spanning trees are even, then every pair of edges on the same fundamental cycle must have the same parity.

keywords.explain: Option D states that within each cycle, all edges share the same parity; this is the necessary and sufficient characterisation when all spanning trees are even.
keywords.example: A triangle with all edges odd has all spanning trees of weight odd+odd = even (and vice versa for all even).

understand.visual_alt: Every spanning tree has even weight, which forces every cycle's edges to be uniformly odd or uniformly even in parity.

given.aim: Determine which statements about edge parities must hold whenever every spanning tree of connected weighted graph G has even total weight.

terms.meaning: Sum of weights of the n-1 edges in the spanning tree; assumed to be even for all spanning trees of G
terms.example: If G is a triangle with weights 2, 4, 6 then both spanning trees have even weight
terms.connects: The parity constraint propagates to individual edges via fundamental cycles

terms.meaning: Replacing one tree edge with a non-tree edge from the fundamental cycle; changes tree weight by w(non-tree) - w(tree)
terms.example: Swapping an odd edge for an even one changes parity, violating the all-even constraint
terms.connects: Forces each fundamental cycle to have uniform edge parity

terms.meaning: Any closed walk in G; every cycle can be expressed as a symmetric difference of fundamental cycles
terms.example: A 4-cycle A-B-C-D-A; option D says all its edge weights must be all odd or all even
terms.connects: Option D is the correct necessary and sufficient condition

given.plan: Use the fundamental-cycle edge-swap argument: swapping a tree edge for a non-tree edge on its fundamental cycle must preserve even parity, so the two edges must share parity. This forces every cycle to have uniform parity. Then check that options A, B, C can be violated.

steps.title: Fundamental-cycle parity argument
steps.formula_raw: T' = T - e + f => w(T') - w(T) = w(f) - w(e)
steps.apply: Let T be any spanning tree with even weight w(T). For any non-tree edge f in G, T + f contains exactly one fundamental cycle C_f. Swapping tree edge e in C_f for f gives T' = T - e + f with w(T') = w(T) + w(f) - w(e). Since w(T') must also be even, w(f) - w(e) must be even, so w(e) and w(f) have the same parity. This holds for every pair of edges in every fundamental cycle.
steps.note: Every cycle in G is a symmetric difference of fundamental cycles, so every cycle inherits the uniform-parity property.

steps.title: Check options A, B, C via counterexample
steps.apply: Consider a graph with a triangle having two even-weight edges and one cycle-completing edge: if the triangle edges are 2, 4, and 2+4=6 (all even), every spanning tree has even weight, but option C (all odd) fails. Similarly a graph with mixed but cycle-consistent parities shows options A and B can fail. Only option D (each cycle has uniform parity) is always satisfied.
steps.note: A graph with edges of weight 2 on one cycle and weight 1 on another cycle can satisfy 'all spanning trees even' while violating options A, B, C.

solution.result: D. In each cycle C in G, either all edges in C have odd weight OR all edges in C have even weight.

questions.formula_note: The spanning-tree edge-swap identity w(T') = w(T) + w(f) - w(e) is the key: preserving even parity forces every pair of edges in a fundamental cycle to share parity, which extends to all cycles.

questions.question:
Let A be the adjacency matrix of a simple undirected graph G. Suppose A is its own inverse. Which one of the following statements is always TRUE?

A. G is a cycle
B. G is a perfect matching
C. G is a complete graph
D. There is no such graph G
questions.answer: B. G is a perfect matching
questions.to_find: Which type of graph has its adjacency matrix equal to its own inverse

understand.plain: If A = A^(-1) then A^2 = I, the identity matrix. The diagonal entries of A^2 equal 1, meaning each vertex has exactly one closed walk of length 2 — i.e., each vertex has exactly one neighbour. Off-diagonal entries of A^2 are 0, meaning no two vertices share a common neighbour. These two conditions together mean G is a disjoint union of edges, which is precisely a perfect matching (assuming n is even). Cycles, complete graphs, and other structures do not satisfy A^2 = I.

keywords.explain: If A is its own inverse, then A^2 = I; this means the diagonal of A^2 is all 1s and off-diagonal is all 0s.
A set of edges with no two edges sharing a vertex, such that every vertex is covered exactly once; the graph is a disjoint union of edges.
keywords.example: On 4 vertices: edges {1-2, 3-4} form a perfect matching; its adjacency matrix satisfies A^2 = I.

keywords.explain: The diagonal of A^2 equals the degree of each vertex; for A^2 = I every vertex must have degree exactly 1.
keywords.example: Degree 1 for all vertices means every vertex is in exactly one edge, giving a perfect matching.

understand.visual_alt: Adjacency matrix A satisfying A squared equals the identity forces every vertex to have degree exactly 1, which is a perfect matching (disjoint union of edges).

given.aim: Find what graph structure forces the adjacency matrix A to satisfy A = A^(-1), i.e., A^2 = I.

terms.meaning: The adjacency matrix is its own inverse; equivalently A^2 = I
terms.connects: Forces strong structural constraints on the graph

terms.meaning: The diagonal of A^2 equals 1 at every position, so deg(i) = 1 for all i
terms.example: Every vertex has exactly one neighbour
terms.connects: Together with (A^2)_{ij}=0 for i!=j, this characterises a perfect matching

terms.meaning: No two distinct vertices share a common neighbour
terms.example: In a matching each vertex has exactly one neighbour with no shared neighbours
terms.connects: Guarantees the edges form a disjoint set with no paths of length 2

given.plan: Translate A = A^(-1) to A^2 = I. Use the adjacency-square-degree formula to read off that each vertex has degree 1. Verify this characterises exactly a perfect matching.

steps.title: Translate the condition to A^2 = I
steps.apply: If A is its own inverse, then A * A = I. So A^2 = I, meaning (A^2)_{ii} = 1 for all i and (A^2)_{ij} = 0 for all i != j.
steps.note: A is symmetric (undirected graph), so A^(-1) = A means A is an involution.

steps.title: Read off degree constraint
steps.apply: (A^2)_{ii} = 1 for all i implies deg(i) = 1 for every vertex. Every vertex has exactly one neighbour. The off-diagonal zero (A^2)_{ij} = 0 for i != j means no two vertices share a common neighbour, consistent with the edges being disjoint. Therefore G is a disjoint union of edges — a perfect matching.
steps.note: A cycle C_4 has deg = 2 so A^2 != I; K_n (n > 2) also fails. Only a perfect matching (1-regular graph) satisfies A^2 = I.

solution.result: B. G is a perfect matching

questions.formula_note: The identity (A^2)_{ii} = deg(i) is the decisive tool: A^2 = I forces all vertex degrees to equal 1, uniquely characterising a perfect matching (1-regular graph).

questions.question:
What is the number of vertices in an undirected connected graph with 27 edges, 6 vertices of degree 2, 3 vertices of degree 4, and remaining vertices of degree 3?

A. 10
B. 11
C. 18
D. 19
questions.to_find: Total number of vertices in the graph

understand.plain: The Handshaking Lemma says the sum of all vertex degrees equals twice the number of edges. Setting up the equation with the given degree groups and solving for the number of remaining (degree-3) vertices gives r = 10, so total vertices = 6 + 3 + 10 = 19.

keywords.explain: In any undirected graph, the sum of all vertex degrees equals twice the number of edges: sum deg(v) = 2|E|.
keywords.example: A graph with 27 edges has sum of degrees = 54.

keywords.explain: The vertices are partitioned into groups by their degree: 6 vertices of degree 2, 3 of degree 4, and r of degree 3.
Setting the degree sum equal to 2*27 = 54 gives r = 10 remaining degree-3 vertices.

understand.visual_alt: Graph with 27 edges partitioned into 6 degree-2, 3 degree-4, and r degree-3 vertices; handshaking gives r=10 and total 19 vertices.

given.aim: Find the total number of vertices given 27 edges and a specific degree distribution.

terms.meaning: The graph has 27 edges, so the total degree sum is 2 * 27 = 54
terms.connects: Provides the equation to solve for the unknown count r

terms.meaning: These contribute 6 * 2 = 12 to the total degree sum
terms.example: Six degree-2 vertices each connect to exactly 2 neighbours
terms.connects: Part of the sum on the left-hand side of the handshaking equation

terms.term: 3 vertices of degree 4 and r vertices of degree 3
terms.meaning: These contribute 3*4 + r*3 = 12 + 3r to the degree sum
terms.example: Three degree-4 vertices each connect to 4 neighbours; r degree-3 vertices each connect to 3
terms.connects: Completes the handshaking equation to solve for r

given.plan: Apply the Handshaking Lemma: sum of all degrees = 2 * 27 = 54. Express the sum using the three degree groups and solve for the unknown count r of degree-3 vertices. Then total vertices = 6 + 3 + r.

steps.title: Apply Handshaking Lemma
steps.formula_raw: sum of deg(v) over all v = 2|E|
steps.apply: 2 * 27 = 54. Setting up: 6*2 + 3*4 + r*3 = 54, where r is the number of remaining (degree-3) vertices.
steps.note: The sum covers every vertex in exactly one group based on its degree.

steps.title: Solve for r and compute total vertices
steps.note: Answer D is 19, matching our solution.

questions.formula_note: The Handshaking Lemma (sum of degrees = 2|E|) is the sole tool needed; setting up the degree-sum equation by groups and solving for r directly gives the vertex count.
